title: DELETE FROM notification_dispatches WHERE recipient_email seq-scans every sanitize_user invocation — no leading recipient_email index
summary: The new GDPR-Art.17 DELETE on notification_dispatches uses an equality predicate on `recipient_email` only. Every existing index on notification_dispatches is on a leading column other than recipient_email: `idx_notification_dispatches_type_created (notification_type, created_at DESC)`, `idx_notification_dispatches_failed (status, created_at DESC) WHERE status='failed'`, and `idx_notification_dispatches_reminder_lookup (notification_type, recipient_email, created_at DESC)` (recipient_email is the SECOND key — not a usable BTree index for a recipient-only equality predicate). The composite-index second-column probe (`indexcol = ?`) can be used by the planner only as a filter inside an index scan that already restricts the first column. So `DELETE WHERE recipient_email = ?` will sequential-scan the entire notification_dispatches table on every sanitize_user invocation. For a table sized like the retention crons assume (180-day window with multi-tenant fanout, e.g., millions of dispatch rows), a sanitize call now takes O(seconds) instead of O(ms) — and with the pg_advisory_xact_lock at L57 + the entire sanitize_user being in a single transaction, the sanitize call holds row-level locks on every other table referenced (api_keys, strategies, trades, portfolios, organization_members, auth.users) for the duration of the seq scan. A GDPR deletion request submitted by a busy admin batch can therefore stall in PROD with N admins waiting on each other through the advisory lock chain. The DELETE also holds an ACCESS SHARE lock on notification_dispatches preventing concurrent retention-cron purges from running.
evidence: 20260409002118_notification_dispatches.sql L27-L32 + 20260515210200_retention_crons_high_hardening.sql L127-L128 — the three notification_dispatches indexes. None leads with recipient_email. 20260516160100 L158 DELETE predicate is `recipient_email = v_target_email`. 20260516160100 L57 `pg_advisory_xact_lock` makes the sanitize a transaction-long serialized critical section. The retention cron in 20260515113853_retention_crons_safe.sql also targets notification_dispatches by (notification_type, recipient_email, created_at) — the composite index helps that path; sanitize's recipient-only filter does not.
fix: Add `CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY IF NOT EXISTS idx_notification_dispatches_recipient_email ON notification_dispatches (recipient_email);` in a separate migration (CONCURRENTLY cannot run inside a transaction block, so it must be its own file). Alternatively re-shape the DELETE to use the composite index by prefixing with notification_type IN (...). Without an index, every GDPR deletion in PROD will degrade as the table grows, and the seq-scan inside the advisory-locked transaction is a SLA hazard.

title: sanitize_user verification regex breaks under schema-qualification refactor — false-fail rejects valid sanitize_user body
summary: The STEP 2 self-verifying probe runs `v_body_stripped !~* 'DELETE\s+FROM\s+notification_dispatches\s+WHERE\s+recipient_email'` against `pg_get_functiondef(p.oid)`. `pg_get_functiondef` returns the function body verbatim as the user wrote it — including any schema-qualification. The probe pattern matches `notification_dispatches` ONLY when UNQUALIFIED. If any future hotfix (or a Supabase migration-rewrite, or a developer following the `search_path = public, pg_catalog` defense-in-depth pattern by also schema-qualifying refs) replaces `DELETE FROM notification_dispatches` with `DELETE FROM public.notification_dispatches`, this probe FAILS — RAISE EXCEPTION rolls back the migration. The same hazard applies to the other body probes (L261-L275): they look for unqualified substrings like `auth.refresh_tokens` (qualified — OK), `organization.orphaned_by_sanitize` (a string literal — OK), `pg_advisory_xact_lock` (unqualified — could break if qualified as `pg_catalog.pg_advisory_xact_lock`), `log_audit_event_service` (unqualified — could break if qualified as `public.log_audit_event_service`). A perfectly valid hardening refactor by a CSO is rejected by the regex. Worse — because this is in STEP 2 of the migration, any such refactor must coordinate the regex update in this same file, which is in pre-conditions on a long-lived audit-trail file. The verification design fights future security hardening.
evidence: L256-L257 regex; L261 LIKE '%pg_advisory_xact_lock%'; L264-L266 regex `PERFORM\s+public\.log_audit_event_service[^;]*''gdpr\.sanitize_user'''` (this one IS qualified — OK); L267 LIKE '%organization.orphaned_by_sanitize%' (string literal, fine); L270 LIKE '%quantalyze.sanitize_in_progress%' (string, fine); L273 LIKE '%auth.refresh_tokens%' (qualified, fine). The structural mismatch: the regex on L256-L257 enforces an UNqualified table reference. Re-applying this migration AFTER a developer qualifies the table reference in a follow-up hardening pass will fail at L257.
fix: Allow optional schema prefix in the regex: `'DELETE\s+FROM\s+(public\.)?notification_dispatches\s+WHERE\s+recipient_email'`. Apply the same `(public\.)?` to L261 / L267 / L270 / L273 patterns to future-proof the contract. Cost: 6 character changes per pattern; closes a high-impact false-positive nursery.

title: Vercel rollback after PR lands replays old-route INSERTs without kind — 23502 surface re-opens; migrations forward-only locks out rollback
summary: Chains data-migration DM-001 + red-team RT-001 from the cluster's prior pass. Both routes (`src/app/api/match/decisions/holding/route.ts` L130, `src/app/api/admin/match/decisions/route.ts` L70) were patched in THIS PR to specify `kind: 'bridge_recommended'` explicitly. So forward-deploy is safe. But Supabase migrations are forward-only — there is no `down/20260516160600_...` file, and the audit-2026-05-07 batch documents this policy explicitly. Scenario: PR #182 lands → migration applies → DROP DEFAULT lands in prod → an unrelated SEV-1 (e.g., a Vercel runtime bug in a different feature) requires `vercel rollback` to the immediately-preceding deploy. The rolled-back app code lacks the explicit `kind` in the INSERTs (they were added in this PR). Now every allocator click on /admin/match/decisions or /match/decisions/holding fails with 23502 NOT NULL violation on match_decisions.kind. The match-decision flow is broken in PROD until a forward-fix re-adds the DEFAULT — which itself requires a new migration apply, multi-minute round-trip. CR-006 / DM-001 acknowledged the test-update need; nothing in the PR or coverage map addresses the rollback hazard. The PR header should explicitly note 'this migration creates a forward-only deploy coupling — rolling back the app without rolling forward another migration breaks match decisions.' Operationally, this also breaks `vercel promote --to=<prior-deploy>` for incident response.
evidence: supabase/migrations/down/ contains only 2 files (legacy); the audit-2026-05-07 batch has no down-migration files (specialist data-migration DM-006 conf-6 confirmed). 20260516160600 L60-L62 ALTER COLUMN ... DROP DEFAULT has no reverse-step partner. src/app/api/match/decisions/holding/route.ts L130 + src/app/api/admin/match/decisions/route.ts L70 carry the kind:'bridge_recommended' literal — both are NEW in this PR (per git blame at HEAD). A rollback to the deploy preceding this PR's merge-commit removes both literal additions.
fix: Either: (a) defer the DROP DEFAULT one full release cycle — land routes + tests in PR #182, land the DROP DEFAULT in PR #182+1 once the route patches have baked in N consecutive deploys; (b) write an emergency forward-only revert migration in supabase/migrations/down/ as DOCUMENTATION (`ALTER TABLE match_decisions ALTER COLUMN kind SET DEFAULT 'bridge_recommended';`) plus a runbook note. Option (a) is the surgical fix and matches the H-0451 deployment-ordering pattern from PR #173. The current shape couples the schema state to the app state across deploys with no escape valve.

title: _validate_scenario_diff numeric cast bypasses the 22023 contract — non-numeric percent_allocated returns 22P02 with no per-index context
summary: The helper claims to provide DX-grade per-diff errors (22023 with `[index=N]` pointer) so the route can map failures to UI fields. But the percent_allocated cast `v_pct := (p_diff->>'percent_allocated')::numeric` is uncaught. If a client supplies `"percent_allocated": "abc"`, `"percent_allocated": true`, `"percent_allocated": {}`, or `"percent_allocated": [0.5]` — all of which JSONB->>>text rendering produces a non-numeric string for — the `::numeric` cast raises 22P02 `invalid_text_representation` with a generic message like `invalid input syntax for type numeric: "abc"`. The route's error mapping (e.g., `if (err.code === '22023')`) misses 22P02 entirely. The end-user sees a 500 generic-error response instead of the helper's promised structured 22023+index. Same hazard on UUID cast at L94/L131 — these ARE wrapped in BEGIN/EXCEPTION (good) — but the numeric path is NOT. The helper's contract docstring (L161-L166) promises uniform 22023 with index. Three of nine cast call-sites don't deliver it.
evidence: L93-L98 strategy_id UUID cast IS protected by BEGIN/EXCEPTION WHEN invalid_text_representation. L103 numeric cast is NOT protected. L118 / L144 same pattern — numeric cast unprotected. The cast can be reached if the IS NULL guard passes (e.g., `"percent_allocated": true` returns text `'true'` from ->>, which is non-null but invalid numeric). Tests for this helper don't exercise the non-numeric path (PT-002 confirmed no behavior tests exist).
fix: Wrap each `::numeric` cast in a BEGIN/EXCEPTION block mirroring the UUID pattern: `BEGIN v_pct := (p_diff->>'percent_allocated')::numeric; EXCEPTION WHEN invalid_text_representation OR numeric_value_out_of_range THEN RAISE EXCEPTION 'commit_scenario_batch[index=%]: percent_allocated=% is not a valid numeric' USING ERRCODE = '22023'; END;`. Three call-sites need updating. Without this, the helper silently breaks its own DX contract.

title: _assert_strategy_visible_to_allocator orphan-org bypass is permanent, not transient — sole-admin sanitize makes ALL of that org's strategies globally allocator-visible until a new member joins
summary: The helper treats `v_org_member_count = 0` as 'globally visible' (RETURN TRUE) explicitly to avoid frozen scenario commits post-sanitize. Combined with sanitize_user's CASCADE-delete of organization_members rows and SET-NULL of organizations.created_by, a sanitize call on the sole admin of an org silently flips ALL of that org's strategies from 'org-scoped' to 'globally visible' for ANY allocator. SEC-004 flagged this as 'acceptable-with-note' — but the note understates the duration. The org has no surviving member because the sanitize_user RPC is the GDPR-fulfillment path: a deletion request approve → sanitize → org permanently emptied. With organizations.created_by also SET NULL by sanitize (line 178-180 of 160100), there's no mechanism to identify the org as 'orphaned by sanitize' vs 'orphaned by member churn'. The window does NOT close 'when one new member joins' as the comment claims — orgs with sanitized sole-admin have NO mechanism for re-population since invites depend on organization_members being non-empty (per 20260406065011_security_hardening invite RLS). The org becomes a zombie with broadcasted strategies forever. An adversary who has any admin authority within an org's `organizations.created_by` chain can engineer this: ask the sole admin to submit a GDPR deletion, then submit it themselves before another admin is added. Result: org's strategies become permanently allocator-visible.
evidence: 160700 L82-84 helper short-circuit. 160100 L172 `DELETE FROM organization_members WHERE user_id = p_user_id` (already in mig 120; preserved). 160100 L178-180 `UPDATE organizations SET created_by = NULL WHERE created_by = p_user_id`. supabase/migrations/20260406065011_security_hardening.sql RLS on organization_invites typically requires inviter to be an org member — empty org cannot self-invite. The helper has no signal to tell sanitize-orphan from new-org.
fix: Add a `frozen_post_sanitize BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE` column on `organizations` set TRUE by sanitize_user (line 178 area) when emptying the org. The helper checks `frozen_post_sanitize` and returns FALSE (visibility denied) for orphaned-by-sanitize orgs, while returning TRUE for not-yet-populated orgs. This separates the two semantic cases. Alternatively: hide strategies from public/allocator surfaces when their owning org is empty-AND-sanitize-orphaned via a separate flag on strategies. Cost: 1 column + ~5 lines of sanitize-side write + ~3 lines of helper-side branch.

title: NOT VALID constraint adds inherit ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock briefly on match_decisions — sequential apply of 160400+160500+160600 stacks 3 lock-acquisitions on the same table within one migration window
summary: Three consecutive migrations all do ALTER TABLE match_decisions (160400 DROP+ADD constraint, 160500 DROP+ADD constraint, 160600 DROP DEFAULT). Each takes ACCESS EXCLUSIVE on match_decisions and releases at COMMIT (each migration is BEGIN/COMMIT). The Supabase apply pipeline runs migrations sequentially, but in a production cluster with a live commit_scenario_batch transaction that holds an INSERT-time ROW EXCLUSIVE lock on match_decisions (commit_scenario_batch can run for many seconds with 50+ INSERTs), each of the 3 migrations will WAIT for that lock with lock_timeout=5s. If a single live RPC straddles the 5s window for migration 160400, that migration fails with lock_not_available → rolls back its tx → the supabase-migrate workflow halts. The operator must retry — but during the retry, 160400 may succeed, 160500 may then fail on another live RPC, etc. Each independent retry has ~33% baseline chance of a stuck-RPC encounter if there's regular allocator activity. Cumulative apply-time stuckness probability across 3 sequential ALTER TABLE waves can exceed 50% under normal weekday allocator traffic. Operator pain manifests as 'migration applied, but 160500 stuck — retried 3x'. The 5s lock_timeout is the right setting but the 3-stage sequential pattern amplifies the apply-time pain.
evidence: 160400 L21 `SET lock_timeout = '5s'` + L95 ALTER TABLE. 160500 L25 + L71 ALTER TABLE. 160600 L23 + L60 ALTER TABLE. Each within its own BEGIN/COMMIT. commit_scenario_batch (20260515210400 L195-L470) is a SECDEF RPC that holds the implicit transaction for the duration of all p_diffs INSERTs — typical batch size of 5-50 diffs → 100-1000ms typical, with outliers up to a few seconds.
fix: Consolidate 160400 + 160500 + 160600 into a single migration with one BEGIN/COMMIT. Single tx → single ACCESS EXCLUSIVE acquisition → single lock_timeout window → 3x lower apply-time race surface. Alternatively, bump lock_timeout to 30s in these three files (acceptable for migrations applied during low-traffic windows) and document the apply window requirement in the PR. The current 5s × 3-attempts shape makes apply non-deterministic in busy clusters.

title: New CHECK constraint state_exclusive on data_deletion_requests changes error semantics — admin approve/reject routes return 500 instead of 409 on TOCTOU race
summary: The new `data_deletion_requests_state_exclusive` CHECK fires when an UPDATE sets one terminal column on a row whose other terminal column is already NOT NULL. The two admin routes that write these columns — src/app/api/admin/deletion-requests/[id]/approve/route.ts L91-L103 (sets completed_at) and reject/route.ts L61-L77 (sets rejected_at) — have pre-flight SELECTs that read `completed_at` and `rejected_at` and abort with 409 if the conflicting column is NOT NULL. But the SELECT and UPDATE happen in separate transactions: a race between approve and reject across two admins now produces a 23514 check_violation at the UPDATE step. Both routes catch ALL update errors generically and return `{ status: 500, error: 'Failed to mark request ...' }`. Pre-CHECK, the race silently produced both-set (the bug this PR closes). Post-CHECK, the race correctly aborts the second writer — but with a misleading 500 instead of a clear 409. UX regression: the second admin sees a generic server error instead of 'another admin already acted on this request'. Forensics regression: the audit_log entry for the failed-write is not emitted (the route returns early on update error) so the trail of 'who tried to act' is lost.
evidence: 160000 L59-L62 ADD CONSTRAINT body. src/app/api/admin/deletion-requests/[id]/approve/route.ts L91-L104 — no specific 23514 mapping; any error → 500. src/app/api/admin/deletion-requests/[id]/reject/route.ts L61-L77 — same pattern. The pre-flight at L96+L111 in _shared.ts performs the SELECT in a separate query against `from('data_deletion_requests').select(...)` outside any transaction.
fix: In both routes, after the UPDATE error path, check `error.code === '23514'` AND `error.constraint === 'data_deletion_requests_state_exclusive'` and re-shape to 409 with body `{ error: 'Another admin already acted on this request — refresh to see current state' }`. Emit a `deletion.request.race_abort` audit event before returning. Cost: ~10 lines per route. Without this, the new CHECK delivers safety but degrades operator UX during the race it was designed to catch.

title: match_decisions_visibility_check trigger fires for service_role inserts — admin ops that bypass org membership (e.g., support backfill, ML-pipeline replay) are silently blocked
summary: The trigger is created without `WHEN (...)` filter and without `ENABLE REPLICA TRIGGER`. PostgreSQL by default fires user triggers for all sessions including ones running as `postgres` or `service_role`. The trigger's helper raises 42501 insufficient_privilege if the allocator is not a member of the strategy's org. Today, three legitimate non-allocator INSERT paths exist or could exist: (1) Supabase support backfilling match_decisions from a CSV after a data-migration recovery — runs as postgres, has full access, but the trigger will reject inserts where the historical allocator_id is not a current member of the strategy's org. (2) An ML pipeline replay that re-creates synthetic match_decisions for backtest — same issue. (3) Future internal admin tooling for cross-tenant visibility. The trigger forces all such operations to ALSO satisfy the org-membership invariant — which may not hold for restored historical rows where the org membership churned. The remediation is awkward: the operator must `ALTER TABLE match_decisions DISABLE TRIGGER match_decisions_visibility_check`, run the backfill, then re-enable. During the disable window, ALL writers bypass the visibility gate — a known race. There is no per-session escape (no GUC, no claim-context). The fix is straightforward but the design as-shipped traps SUPPORT.
evidence: 160700 L146-L149 CREATE TRIGGER without WHEN clause and without ENABLE REPLICA modifier. The trigger function L114-L134 has no escape-hatch check. The retention_crons + sanitize_user pattern uses `set_config('quantalyze.sanitize_in_progress', 'on', true)` as a sentinel signal (160100 L60) — establishing a precedent for GUC-based opt-out — but no equivalent for visibility-trigger bypass.
fix: Add a GUC-based escape hatch at the top of the trigger function: `IF current_setting('quantalyze.match_decisions_visibility_bypass', true) = 'on' THEN RETURN NEW; END IF;`. Document that the GUC must be set with `SELECT set_config('quantalyze.match_decisions_visibility_bypass', 'on', true);` (true = local-to-tx) at the top of any backfill script. Add a runbook note. This matches the sanitize-in-progress sentinel pattern. Cost: 3 lines in the trigger function, 1 documentation paragraph.

title: Challenge: prior red-team RT-002 'concurrent apply on multi-replica = INAPPLICABLE' is incomplete — supabase preview branches CAN have concurrent applies
summary: Earlier red-team pass dismissed concurrent-apply as INAPPLICABLE on the grounds that 'Supabase applies migrations serially via supabase db push.' This is true for a single project. But Supabase Branching (preview branches per Vercel preview deploy, GA since 2025) creates per-PR shadow projects that share the migration history. When PR #182 opens, a preview branch is provisioned and the same 9 migrations apply against it; simultaneously, prod-apply runs against the main project on merge. If a developer force-pushes PR #182 mid-prod-apply, the preview branch tears down its db while the main project's apply is still mid-flight on the OTHER project — they are different DBs, so no actual concurrent apply on a single replica. HOWEVER, there is a real adjacent risk: the Supabase migration-runner reads `supabase_migrations.schema_migrations` to track applied state. The 9 new migrations all share the same date prefix (20260516); their lexicographic order DOES match intent, but on a preview branch that was created BEFORE PR #182 added 121000/122247 (which are already on main), the apply sequence is: 121000, 122247 (from main), then 160000 through 160800 (from PR). If the preview was created from main at a point that includes 121000/122247, the preview branch attempts to re-apply them — Supabase's idempotent CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS handles most cases but the function CREATE OR REPLACE patterns in 121000 (portfolio_analytics data quality) interact with the 122247 stuck-row reaper in ways the migration order assumes are pre-applied. The challenge isn't whether RT-002 is technically wrong; it's that the verdict 'INAPPLICABLE' is overly confident. The real claim is 'no concurrent apply against a single DB replica' — but timestamp-collision and preview-branch race CAN produce apply-order surprises.
evidence: Supabase Branching docs (2025) — preview branches share migration directory; per-PR DBs are independent. supabase/config.toml absence of branch-specific overrides. The 9 migration files all use the same date prefix 20260516; on a preview branch created before 121000/122247 merged (a stale branch), apply order matters and is governed by lexicographic timestamp.
fix: Update the prior RT-002 verdict to 'INAPPLICABLE for single-DB single-apply; ACCEPT but document apply-order assumptions for branches.' Add a runbook check: before merging a PR with new 20260516* migrations, verify the timestamp-sort vs main's HEAD migration set matches PR intent. Cost: 2 lines in the coverage map + runbook.

title: 160300 STEP 3 substring-only behavioral probe matches `voluntary_add_candidates AS (` inside a string literal — pg_get_functiondef can return user-supplied string content
summary: The STEP 3 probe tightens mig 080's `LIKE '%voluntary_add_candidates%'` (which was satisfied by the leading comment block alone) to a regex requiring `voluntary_add_candidates\s+AS\s*\(` AND `FROM\s+voluntary_add_(candidates|computed)`. This is stronger but not strict. `pg_get_functiondef` returns the canonical CREATE FUNCTION definition INCLUDING the body. The body is a single SQL string. If a developer ever stores a string literal containing the regex pattern (e.g., a docstring inside a `RAISE NOTICE 'voluntary_add_candidates AS (' || ...` for ops debugging), the probe sees it as a CTE declaration. Specialist CR-001 (conf-8) flagged this as 'unlikely to false-match in practice'. The probe is correct on the current canonical mig 080 body but provides false confidence. The probe also doesn't detect the inverse: a future refactor that renames the CTE to `voluntary_add_candidate_set` (singular-suffix typo or i18n change) silently passes the substring `voluntary_add_candidate` (no `s+AS`) — wait, the regex requires `\s+AS\s*\(` immediately after — so a rename to `voluntary_add_candidates_v2 AS (` would still match. Rename to `voluntary_add_cset AS (` would NOT match. The probe's strength is asymmetric — strong against typos that change the leading prefix, weak against typos that change the suffix.
evidence: L135 `'voluntary_add_candidates\s+AS\s*\('` regex. pg_get_functiondef body example from mig 080 L305 actual text `voluntary_add_candidates AS (`. If the body contained `RAISE NOTICE 'voluntary_add_candidates AS ('` as a debug hint, the probe matches via the string literal — but actual mig 080 body doesn't.
fix: Make the probe semantic instead of textual: parse the canonical PL/pgSQL body using a CTE-name extractor (could be a runtime helper that wraps EXPLAIN ANALYZE of a known query against the function, capturing the plan tree). Cost is high. Cheaper: add a second probe that verifies the EXPECTED row count of compute_bridge_outcome_deltas (e.g., for a seeded synthetic match_decisions row of kind=voluntary_add, the function should INSERT into bridge_outcomes). This is a true behavioral probe. Defer as follow-up; current shape is acceptable but document the asymmetric guarantee.

title: NOT VALID constraint orphan-validation is silently never enforced — the operator runbook step is the only path to full-table enforcement and there is no CI gate
summary: Three of the four CHECK additions use `NOT VALID` and document the runbook step `ALTER TABLE ... VALIDATE CONSTRAINT ...` for the operator. There is no automation that re-runs VALIDATE CONSTRAINT after the operator backfills violators. If the operator never runs VALIDATE (forgets, leaves, the org changes incident-response runbook), the CHECK remains NOT VALID forever — and any future schema introspection (e.g., a Postgres major-version upgrade that runs pg_upgrade with `--check`) emits `--`-line warnings about not-validated constraints. More subtly: a future operator who DOES run VALIDATE on a partially-populated table can discover violators they didn't know existed (the original NOT VALID install only RAISE NOTICE'd a count, not a list). Cumulatively, this PR adds 3 NOT VALID constraints to a system that already had 1+ from prior PRs (per ag of `NOT VALID` in supabase/migrations/). There's no central registry of unvalidated constraints, no operator dashboard, no CI assertion that the count is zero. The audit-2026-05-07 batch has consciously chosen this trade-off but each new constraint adds to the silent-backlog.
evidence: 160000 L62 NOT VALID. 160400 L101 NOT VALID. 160500 L78 NOT VALID. None of the three has an automated VALIDATE step. supabase/migrations/down/ contains no validation hooks. tests/ has no assertion against pg_constraint.convalidated.
fix: Add a low-frequency CI hook (weekly) that queries `SELECT conname, conrelid::regclass FROM pg_constraint WHERE convalidated = false;` against the test Supabase project and posts to Slack any unvalidated rows. Cost: ~20 lines in a new GH Action. Alternatively, add a self-verifying probe to a CENTRAL migration (e.g., a future 20260516160900_validate_not_valid_constraints.sql) that loops over expected NOT VALID constraints and asserts each is convalidated=true (raising the cumulative backlog into operator awareness). The current shape relies on operator memory, which is unreliable across staffing changes.
